# Summary Case Report

## Atlantic Gateway Infrastructure Holdings B.V. v. The Republic of [Redacted]

**Investment Arbitration Reporter — Case Summary Series**
**Issue No. 412, January 2026**
**Reporter: Magdalena Reyes-Holm**

---

## Bibliographic Note

This summary case report covers the final award rendered by a tribunal of the International Chamber of Commerce in ICC Case No. 26,4xx/AGF, *Atlantic Gateway Infrastructure Holdings B.V. v. The Republic of [Redacted]*, dated 14 November 2023.

The summary is prepared from the redacted text of the final award (excerpt) released by the parties in mid-2024 and from supplementary procedural documents made available through the Reporter's standard sources. The Reporter has not had access to the full text of the award, and the summary should be read with that caveat.

The case is, in the view of the editors of the Investment Arbitration Reporter, of substantial doctrinal interest in three respects: (i) the tribunal's reasoning on the relationship between regulatory acts *jure imperii* and contractually delimited dispute-resolution clauses; (ii) the tribunal's treatment of stabilisation clauses in concession agreements as incorporating public-international-law principles; and (iii) the tribunal's quantum methodology, which awarded a substantial but reduced damages figure relative to the principal claim.

---

## I. Background

---

## I. Background

The dispute arose out of a 30-year build-operate-transfer concession (the "Concession") granted in 2014 to the local project company of Atlantic Gateway Infrastructure Holdings B.V., a Netherlands-incorporated entity. The Concession concerned the design, financing, construction, and operation of a deepwater container terminal on the Atlantic coast of the Respondent State.

The substantive dispute concerned the lawfulness of two ministerial orders — issued in March 2021 and July 2021 (together, the "Disputed Orders") — which between them suspended the Claimant's exclusive berthing rights at the terminal and re-allocated approximately 38 per cent of projected throughput to a competing state-controlled port operator.

Atlantic Gateway commenced ICC arbitration on 9 February 2022 under Article 41 of the Concession, claiming damages in the principal sum of EUR 612.4 million together with pre- and post-award interest, costs, and a declaration that the Disputed Orders breached the stabilisation clause at Article 27 of the Concession.

---

## II. The Tribunal

The tribunal was composed of:

- **Dr. Amara N. Okonkwo** (presiding arbitrator) — Nigerian national, London- and Lagos-based independent arbitrator.
- **Professor Henri Lagarde** (co-arbitrator appointed by the Claimant) — French national, professor of international economic law.
- **Mr. Sipho Dlamini SC** (co-arbitrator appointed by the Respondent) — South African senior counsel.

The seat of arbitration was Paris, France. The governing law was the Concession Agreement of 2014; subsidiarily, the laws of the Respondent State; and, where applicable, public international law principles incorporated by reference into Article 27 of the Concession.

---

## III. Procedural History

The tribunal was constituted on 22 June 2022. Procedural Order No. 1 was issued on 19 July 2022 following a case-management conference held by videoconference. A combined hearing on jurisdiction and merits was held in Paris from 6 to 15 May 2023. Post-hearing briefs were filed on 4 August 2023. The proceedings were declared closed under Article 27 of the 2021 ICC Rules on 22 September 2023. The award was issued in Paris on 14 November 2023.

The Respondent's principal jurisdictional objection was rejected in the tribunal's Decision on Jurisdiction of 12 December 2022. That decision, which was incorporated into the final award by reference, held that the Concession's broad arbitration clause — "any dispute *arising out of or in connection with* this Agreement" — captured regulatory acts that directly impaired the Claimant's contractual entitlements, regardless of their formal characterisation under the domestic law of the Respondent State as acts *jure imperii*.

The Reporter notes that the jurisdictional decision is itself of interest as a reasoned application of the principle that the formal categorisation of an act as sovereign or commercial under domestic law is not dispositive of its characterisation for the purposes of a contractual arbitration clause. The tribunal's reasoning on this point is consistent with the line of ICC awards beginning with the well-known *SPP v. Egypt* and *Pyramids* line of cases.

---

## IV. Findings on Breach

The tribunal found, by majority (Mr. Dlamini SC dissenting in part), that the Disputed Orders — taken together — constituted breaches of three provisions of the Concession:

The tribunal rejected the Respondent's principal substantive defence — that the Disputed Orders constituted a *bona fide* exercise of police power necessary to address port congestion. The contemporaneous documentary record, and in particular an inter-ministerial memorandum dated 11 February 2021 (admitted as Exhibit R-117), demonstrated to the tribunal's satisfaction that the decisive motivation behind the Orders was the protection of a competing state-owned port operator, rather than any genuine concern with congestion management.

The Reporter highlights the tribunal's discussion of Exhibit R-117 as an instructive example of how contemporaneous internal government documents, when produced in arbitration, can determine the outcome on the question of regulatory motive. The memorandum, on the tribunal's reading, simply could not be reconciled with the Respondent's *bona fide* police-power defence.

The tribunal's analysis of the stabilisation clause, contained at paragraphs 142 through 311 of the award (which are not included in the publicly released excerpt), is reported by counsel sources to have been notably substantive. The clause, according to those sources, was read by the tribunal as incorporating general principles of public international law concerning the protection of legitimate expectations and the obligation of the State to act in good faith in respect of contractual undertakings. We hope to publish a fuller analytical note on the tribunal's stabilisation-clause reasoning when the full text of the award becomes available.

---

## V. Quantum

---

## V. Quantum

The tribunal awarded the Claimant the sum of **EUR 418.9 million** in damages, together with compound interest at six-month EURIBOR plus 2 per cent from 1 August 2021 until payment in full.

The amount awarded represented approximately 68 per cent of the principal sum claimed. The Reporter understands that the discount reflects the tribunal's modifications to the Claimant's discounted-cash-flow model, including in particular: (i) a higher discount rate than that proposed by the Claimant's quantum experts; (ii) a more conservative throughput assumption for the diverted volume; and (iii) a temporal limitation on certain elements of the lost-profits claim. The full reasoning of the tribunal on quantum is contained in the omitted paragraphs of the award.

The award also ordered the Respondent to bear 70 per cent of the costs of the arbitration (including the tribunal's fees and expenses, the ICC administrative charge, and the Claimant's reasonable legal costs).

---

## VI. The Dissent

Mr. Dlamini SC's partial dissent — which has not been publicly released — is understood from sources close to the proceedings to have focused on the tribunal's quantum analysis and, in particular, on the treatment of the Claimant's lost-profits claim for the post-2030 period.

The Reporter has not seen the dissent and cannot summarise it further. We hope to publish a separate note on the dissent if and when it becomes available.

---

## VII. Significance

In the Reporter's assessment, the award is of significance for the following reasons:

---

## VII. Significance

In the Reporter's assessment, the award is of significance for the following reasons:

1. **Stabilisation clause as a vehicle for public international law principles.** The tribunal's treatment of Article 27 of the Concession as incorporating general principles of public international law (rather than relying solely on the domestic law of the Respondent State or on the four corners of the contract) is a relatively unusual approach in ICC commercial arbitration, and may prove influential in future cases involving concession agreements with stabilisation provisions.

2. **Regulatory act / contractual breach.** The tribunal's finding that an act formally characterised as a sovereign regulatory act under domestic law may nonetheless constitute a contractual breach for the purposes of an arbitration clause has implications for the broader debate about the boundary between investment-treaty and commercial arbitration.

3. **Quantum methodology.** The tribunal's substantial but reduced damages award reflects what appears to be a careful and well-reasoned quantum analysis, and may serve as a useful reference point for tribunals in future infrastructure-concession cases.

4. **The contemporaneous documentary record.** The decisive role of the inter-ministerial memorandum (Exhibit R-117) underscores the importance of document-production practice in cases involving disputed regulatory motives.

---

## VIII. Post-Award Procedure

The Reporter understands that the Respondent State has applied to the French courts for annulment of the award under Article 1520 of the French Code of Civil Procedure. The annulment application is currently pending before the Paris Court of Appeal. We will report further once a decision is rendered.

There has, as of the date of this report, been no public announcement of voluntary compliance with the award.

---

According to publicly available sources:

## X. About This Series

---

## X. About This Series

The Investment Arbitration Reporter's *Summary Case Report* series provides timely summaries of investment-treaty and major commercial arbitration awards as they enter the public domain. The series is intended to facilitate the work of practitioners, academics, and policy commentators in keeping abreast of significant arbitration developments.

Summaries are prepared from public sources, including redacted award texts, party press releases, court filings in annulment or enforcement proceedings, and supplementary information made available to the Reporter on a confidential basis.

The Reporter does not provide legal advice and does not warrant the accuracy of any particular summary. Readers should consult the original award text where it is available, and should consult qualified counsel before relying on any summary in connection with any actual or potential legal matter.

---

## XI. Editor's Note: Reading This Award Alongside the Original Excerpt

---

## XI. Editor's Note: Reading This Award Alongside the Original Excerpt

Readers who have access to the redacted excerpt of the final award released by the parties in mid-2024 will note that the present summary follows the structure of that excerpt closely, including its identification of the parties, the tribunal composition, the seat of arbitration, the governing law, the substantive findings on breach, and the quantum disposition. The Reporter has reordered and condensed certain elements for clarity, and has supplemented the publicly available text with information drawn from procedural orders, party submissions, and confidential discussions with counsel close to the proceedings.

The principal differences between this summary and the redacted excerpt are: (i) this summary expands on the procedural history, drawing on information available to the Reporter beyond the text of the award itself; (ii) this summary provides analytical commentary on the significance of the award that is not contained in the award itself; and (iii) this summary contextualises the award within the broader stream of ICC investment-related awards in a way that the parties' release naturally does not.

We hope that the summary will be of use to subscribers. As always, we welcome correspondence from practitioners with additional information bearing on cases summarised in this series, and we are happy to publish corrections, clarifications, and supplementary notes in subsequent issues.

---

## Appendix A — Cross-References

The following ICC and other commercial arbitration awards have been cited or relied upon, by either the tribunal or the parties, in connection with the principal substantive findings in the case:

- *Société Générale de l'Industrie du Papier (RAKTA) v. The Bahamas* (ICC Case No. 8385) — on the relationship between regulatory measures and contractual undertakings.
- *Hubco v. WAPDA* (ICC Case No. 9899) — on stabilisation provisions in power-sector concessions and the boundary between regulatory and contractual obligations.
- *Wena Hotels Ltd. v. Arab Republic of Egypt* (ICSID Case No. ARB/98/4) — on the protection of contractually agreed exclusivity provisions in a state concession.
- *SGS Société Générale de Surveillance S.A. v. Republic of the Philippines* (ICSID Case No. ARB/02/6) — on the contractual versus treaty characterisation of state acts in concession contexts.
- *Patrick Mitchell v. Democratic Republic of the Congo* (ICSID Case No. ARB/99/7), Decision on Annulment, 1 November 2006 — on the boundary between regulatory and contractual breaches.
- *Compañía de Aguas del Aconquija S.A. and Vivendi Universal v. Argentine Republic* (ICSID Case No. ARB/97/3), Decision on Annulment, 3 July 2002 — on the parallel applicability of contractual and treaty standards.

These cross-references are provided for the convenience of readers and are not exhaustive of the authorities considered by the tribunal.

## Appendix D — Glossary of Key Terms

## Appendix D — Glossary of Key Terms

For the benefit of readers less familiar with the terminology of investment arbitration, the following short glossary is provided.

- **Concession agreement.** A long-term contract between a state (or state entity) and a private investor under which the investor is granted the right to construct, operate, and (often) eventually transfer back to the state, a piece of infrastructure or a productive asset.

- **Stabilisation clause.** A contractual provision in a long-term concession agreement under which the state undertakes that adverse subsequent changes in the legal or regulatory framework will not be applied to the investor, or that the investor will be compensated for any such adverse changes. Stabilisation clauses come in many forms; the form considered in this case incorporated public international law principles in respect of the stabilisation undertaking.

- ***Jure imperii*.** Latin phrase meaning "by sovereign right." Refers to acts performed by a state in its sovereign capacity, as distinguished from acts performed in a commercial capacity (*jure gestionis*). The distinction is important in a number of areas of international law, including state immunity from suit.

- ***Bona fide*.** Latin phrase meaning "in good faith." Often used to characterise a regulatory act undertaken for genuine public-interest reasons, as distinguished from one undertaken for protectionist or other illegitimate reasons.

- **Article 27 of the ICC Rules (2021).** The provision of the 2021 ICC Arbitration Rules governing the closing of proceedings.

- **Annulment.** A post-award procedure under which the validity of an arbitration award may be challenged before a national court (in the case of commercial awards) or, in the case of ICSID Convention awards, before an ad hoc Committee. The grounds for annulment are typically limited and procedural rather than substantive.

- **EURIBOR.** Euro Interbank Offered Rate. A benchmark interest rate at which Eurozone banks lend to one another, frequently used as a reference rate for interest on damages awards in Eurozone-denominated arbitrations.

## Appendix E — Reader Correspondence

The Investment Arbitration Reporter welcomes correspondence from readers in respect of the case summarised in this report.

Practitioners who have additional information bearing on the case — including, in particular, on the post-award annulment proceedings before the Paris Court of Appeal — are invited to contact the Reporter through the confidential submission portal on the Reporter's website.

Substantive comments and clarifications received from readers will, where appropriate, be considered for publication in a future issue, with attribution or without attribution at the reader's preference.

The Reporter also welcomes corrections of factual errors and is committed to publishing corrections of any errors that may come to its attention.

A more detailed analysis of this case, including a fuller treatment of the tribunal's stabilisation-clause reasoning and the quantum analysis, is forthcoming in a future issue of the Reporter's quarterly *Long Read* series.

# Gray's Inn Square Chambers

## Members

*Chambers website — Members directory*
*Last updated: 11 February 2026*

---

Gray's Inn Square Chambers is a specialist commercial set, founded in 1947, with particular strengths in international commercial litigation, public international law, energy and natural resources disputes, international arbitration, and complex regulatory work. We are one of the longest-established sets specialising in international commercial practice in London, and our members appear regularly before courts and tribunals in the United Kingdom and across more than thirty other jurisdictions.

This page lists our full members and door tenants. For details of our practice management team, mini-pupillage, pupillage applications, and chambers operations, please use the navigation menu above.

---

## Full Members — King's Counsel

*Called 1982, Silk 2004*

## Full Members — King's Counsel

### Sir James Hartwell KC

*Called 1982, Silk 2004*

Sir James is widely recognised as one of the leading commercial silks of his generation. His practice spans international commercial litigation, banking and financial services disputes, civil fraud, and international commercial arbitration. He has appeared in many of the leading commercial cases in the English courts over the past two decades, including the Court of Appeal proceedings in *Sokolov v. Lambeth Wharf Holdings* [2019] EWCA Civ 412 and the Supreme Court appeal in *Phoenix Resources Ltd v. Northumbria Investments plc* [2022] UKSC 8. He is currently instructed in a substantial LCIA arbitration arising out of a $1.4 billion structured-finance dispute between two Eastern European parties.

Sir James was knighted in the 2023 New Year Honours for services to international commercial law and to legal education in the Commonwealth.

### Patricia Allenby KC

*Called 1986, Silk 2007*

Ms. Allenby's practice focuses on civil fraud, asset tracing, and international commercial arbitration. She has particular experience in cases involving the British Virgin Islands, the Cayman Islands, Jersey, and Guernsey, and is regularly instructed in proceedings before the courts of those jurisdictions. She has acted as arbitrator in over forty institutional arbitrations under the LCIA, ICC, SCC, and HKIAC Rules, and is on the Singapore International Arbitration Centre's panel of arbitrators.

She is the Standing Counsel to the Government of the British Virgin Islands in civil-recovery matters and has been a member of the Bar Council's International Committee since 2018.

### Sir Roderick MacAllister KC

*Called 1979, Silk 2001*

Sir Roderick's practice covers public international law, international commercial arbitration, and energy and natural resources disputes. He has appeared in proceedings before the International Court of Justice (as junior counsel for the Republic of Botswana in the *Kasikili/Sedudu Island* case), the International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea, the Iran–United States Claims Tribunal, and the ICSID Annulment Committee in *Patrick Mitchell v. Democratic Republic of Congo*.

He sits as arbitrator under the rules of all the major institutions and is currently presiding over four investment-treaty arbitrations. He was knighted in 2018 for services to international law.

### Bernadette Otieno KC

*Called 1991, Silk 2012*

Ms. Otieno's practice covers commercial litigation, international arbitration, and public international law, with a particular focus on African energy and mining disputes. She has been counsel in numerous investment-treaty arbitrations against African respondent States, acting for both claimants and respondents.

She is a member of the editorial board of the *Journal of African International Arbitration* and a co-author of the chapter on Anglophone Africa in the most recent edition of *International Commercial Arbitration* (Born, Sixth Edition).

### Daniel Markham KC

*Called 1988, Silk 2009*

Mr. Markham's practice covers international commercial arbitration, energy disputes, and international investment law. He has appeared as counsel in over sixty international arbitrations and as arbitrator in over thirty. He is a former Vice President of the LCIA Court and currently sits on the ICC Commission on Arbitration and ADR.

His current matters include the underlying proceedings in *Northvale Petroleum International v. Republic of [redacted]* (ICSID Case No. ARB/22/41) and a major LCIA arbitration arising out of a $3.2 billion gas-pipeline construction project in Central Asia.

Chambers offers up to three twelve-month pupillage places each year, with a primary focus on candidates intending to develop practices in international commercial arbitration, commercial litigation, and public international law. Pupils receive a Pupillage Award of £85,000 (twelve months) and are guaranteed first six months of guided pupillage with a designated pupil supervisor. Tenancy decisions are made in or around month nine of the second six.

We are committed to recruiting from the widest possible pool of candidates. # ICSID Caseload — Statistics

## Special Issue 2023

**International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes**
**Washington, D.C. — Issue 2023-2**

*This issue presents the ICSID caseload statistics for the calendar year 2023, with comparative data for the preceding ten-year period. The data reflects cases registered through 31 December 2023 under the ICSID Convention and the Additional Facility Rules.*

---

## Executive Summary

In 2023, ICSID registered 56 new cases — 47 under the ICSID Convention and 9 under the Additional Facility Rules. This represents a modest decrease of 6.7 per cent from the 60 cases registered in 2022, but remains broadly consistent with the average annual registration volume of 58.4 cases for the five-year period 2019–2023.

Total cases administered by ICSID — including those registered in prior years and remaining pending at any point during 2023 — reached an all-time high of 354 at year-end. Forty-two cases were concluded during the year, by award, settlement, or discontinuance.

The 2023 caseload reflects continued geographic and sectoral diversification, with notable growth in registrations from sub-Saharan Africa (eight cases, against a ten-year annual average of 4.3) and from the renewable-energy and digital-infrastructure sectors.

---

## Section 1 — Cases Registered by Year

The peak year remains 2021, when 69 cases were registered — driven principally by registrations under the Energy Charter Treaty and by a cluster of cases against a single respondent State concerning regulatory changes to the renewable-energy support regime.

### 4.2 Notes on Sectoral Trends

The 2023 caseload reflects a continued shift in the energy sector toward renewable rather than conventional power generation as the principal source of investment-treaty disputes. Of the twelve energy-sector cases registered in 2023, nine concerned solar, wind, or hybrid renewable projects; only three concerned conventional fossil-fuel power generation.

The mining caseload remains substantial, with a continuing focus on Latin American and African respondent States. Of the six mining-sector cases registered in 2023, four involved disputes over the suspension, revocation, or non-renewal of exploration or exploitation permits. The remaining two involved disputes over the application of resource-rent and royalty regimes.

Cases concerning digital infrastructure — particularly subsea cable systems, data centres, and telecommunications licensing — accounted for four of the 2023 registrations, up from one such case in 2018.

## Section 8 — Procedural Innovations and Reform

### 8.1 Implementation of the 2022 ICSID Arbitration Rules

The 2022 ICSID Arbitration Rules took effect on 1 July 2022. As of 31 December 2023, 87 cases had been registered under the 2022 Rules, of which 56 were registered in 2023.

Tribunals operating under the 2022 Rules have made increased use of the new procedural mechanisms, including in particular:

Tribunals operating under the 2022 Rules have made increased use of the new procedural mechanisms, including in particular:

- Expedited procedures under Chapter XII (5 cases as of 31 December 2023)
- Bifurcation under Rule 44 (32 cases in which bifurcation was granted, out of 47 in which it was requested)
- Decisions on manifestly without legal merit objections under Rule 41 (8 such decisions rendered in 2023, all dismissing the objection)

### 8.2 Mediation Caseload

The ICSID Mediation Rules, adopted in 2022, have generated modest but growing activity. As of 31 December 2023, the Centre had administered 7 mediation proceedings, of which 4 were registered in 2023. Three of these resulted in settlement.

## Section 9 — Financial and Administrative Notes

### 9.1 Average Duration of Proceedings

The average duration of proceedings concluded by award in 2023 was 4.3 years from registration to dispatch of the award. This is broadly in line with the average for the five-year period 2019–2023 (4.4 years) and reflects continuing Centre and tribunal attention to procedural efficiency.

### 9.2 Costs

The aggregate amount of fees and expenses approved by the Centre in 2023 in connection with arbitrator services was USD 79.4 million, up from USD 67.2 million in 2022. The increase reflects both the growth in active caseload and an upward adjustment in the standard arbitrator fee scale that took effect on 1 January 2023.

### 9.3 Advance Payment Arrears

### 9.3 Advance Payment Arrears

As at 31 December 2023, 18 cases had outstanding advance-payment arrears, of which 6 had been suspended pursuant to Administrative and Financial Regulation 14(3)(d). The Centre continues to monitor advance-payment arrears closely and to engage with parties to bring matters to compliance where possible.

---

## Section 10 — Methodological Notes

The data in this report is derived from the Centre's internal case management system and reflects information available to the Secretariat as of 31 December 2023. Cases may be reclassified upon the receipt of further information, and the figures presented here may, in future publications, be revised in minor respects.

Cases are counted by registration: a case that has been registered but later discontinued, settled, or otherwise concluded is counted as a registered case in the year of registration and as a concluded case in the year of conclusion. Cases involving multiple respondents are counted as a single case.

Sectoral classification follows the ICSID internal sectoral taxonomy, which is broadly aligned with the United Nations International Standard Industrial Classification (ISIC) Rev. 4. The Centre acknowledges that some cases involve investments across multiple sectors; in such cases, the primary sector is recorded.

Geographic classification of respondent States follows the World Bank country and lending groups taxonomy. Geographic classification of arbitrator nationality follows the arbitrator's principal nationality as recorded in the appointment.

# "The treaty network is not the problem — the drafting is."

*An interview with Dr. Amara Okonkwo*
*Conducted for the* Journal of African Dispute Resolution*, Vol. 6, Issue 2 (Spring 2024). Interview held by videoconference, 12 February 2024. Lightly edited for clarity.*

---

**JADR: You've been on the LCIA Court for some time now. How has your view of investor-state arbitration in Africa evolved?**

---

**JADR: You've been on the LCIA Court for some time now. How has your view of investor-state arbitration in Africa evolved?**

**Okonkwo:** I've been on the Court for — let me think — about twelve years now, which is longer than it feels. When I started, the conversation in African capitals was overwhelmingly defensive: "How do we get out of these treaties? How do we limit exposure?" That has shifted. The conversation now is much more sophisticated. States want to know how to use the system, not just survive it. South Africa terminated; Tanzania terminated; but most of the continent has decided the better path is to renegotiate and to draft better. I agree with that view. The treaty network is not the problem — the drafting is.

**JADR: You've sat in both commercial and investor-state matters. Do you have a preference?**

**Okonkwo:** Honestly, no. The intellectual work is different. A commercial case is usually about the discipline of the contractual record; an investment case is about the discipline of the regulatory record and the customary international law overlay. Both reward careful reading. I do think the African infrastructure docket sits interestingly at the boundary — concession disputes often raise both contract and treaty arguments in the same proceeding. The Atlantic Gateway matter I recently chaired had that flavour, although obviously I cannot say more.

**JADR: Practitioners sometimes complain about the cost of African-seated arbitration. What's your reaction?**

**JADR: Practitioners sometimes complain about the cost of African-seated arbitration. What's your reaction?**

**Okonkwo:** Two reactions. First, the cost of African-seated arbitration is, in my experience, lower than London or Paris, not higher — the institutional fees at CRCICA and the LCA are competitive, and local counsel rates in Lagos or Nairobi are a fraction of Magic Circle rates. Second, arbitrator fees are arbitrator fees; the seat does not change them very much. I charge GBP 750 an hour whether I'm sitting in London or Lagos, and that's broadly in line with the market for someone at my stage. If a tribunal is expensive, it's usually because the case is being run inefficiently, not because of geography.

**JADR: You did your doctorate in Geneva. Did you ever consider an academic career?**

**Okonkwo:** I did. I had an offer from Leiden in 2013 and one from SOAS a few years later. I turned both down. I enjoy writing — I try to publish one substantial piece a year — but I would have missed the practice. There is something about being in the room when a tribunal is actually deciding a case that a classroom cannot replicate.

**JADR: Final question — what should young African arbitration lawyers focus on?**

**JADR: Final question — what should young African arbitration lawyers focus on?**

**Okonkwo:** Languages, first. If you can work in English, French, and Portuguese, you can take instructions from any government on the continent. Second, learn the OHADA system properly — most non-OHADA lawyers, including me when I started, underestimate it. And third, sit in real cases as a tribunal secretary before you ever try to get appointed. There is no shortcut to seeing how it actually works.

# Okonkwo chairs landmark West African port award; sights set on AfCFTA disputes

*African Law & Business — 28 November 2023*
*By Tunde Adebayo, Senior Reporter*

A three-member ICC tribunal chaired by Dr Amara Okonkwo has handed down what practitioners are already calling one of the largest concession awards involving a sub-Saharan African state in the past decade, ordering a coastal republic to pay EUR 418.9 million to a Dutch-incorporated infrastructure consortium over the suspension of exclusive berthing rights at a deepwater container port.

The award, dated 14 November and seated in Paris, follows nearly two years of proceedings and a ten-day hearing held in May. Although the parties and the respondent state remain confidential under ICC practice, three sources familiar with the matter confirmed the principal figures and the composition of the tribunal, which also included Henri Lagarde of the Paris Bar and Sipho Dlamini SC of Johannesburg.

Okonkwo, who divides her practice between London and Lagos, has steadily built one of the most active African-facing arbitration books on the continent. Called to the Nigerian Bar in 2004 and admitted as a solicitor in England and Wales eight years later, she completed her doctorate in international law at the University of Geneva in 2012 under Professor Marcelo Kohen, with a thesis on the limits of investor-state arbitration in sub-Saharan Africa that is still widely cited.

"Amara is unusual in that she's equally credible to a European GC and to an African ministry of justice," said one London-based partner who has appeared before her twice. "She doesn't treat the African context as exotic — it's her home jurisdiction."

She joined the LCIA Court in 2016 and has sat on the ICC's Africa Commission since 2019. The Federal Republic of Nigeria designated her to the ICSID Panel of Arbitrators in 2018, a six-year appointment that lapsed earlier this year.

The Atlantic Gateway matter is the second large infrastructure award Okonkwo has chaired in the past 18 months. She was also presiding arbitrator in a CRCICA construction dispute concerning a rail extension in North Africa, although the outcome of that proceeding has not been made public.

In a short statement to *African Law & Business*, Okonkwo declined to discuss the award itself, citing confidentiality, but noted that "the tribunal worked hard to ensure that both the contractual record and the regulatory context received the analytical weight they deserved." She added that she expected an increasing number of disputes under the African Continental Free Trade Area framework to reach institutional arbitration over the next five years, "particularly where states have layered concession regimes on top of older investment treaties."

Okonkwo is a fellow of the Chartered Institute of Arbitrators and a founding council member of the African Arbitration Association. She is listed as a Thought Leader in *Who's Who Legal: Arbitration*.

## I. Introduction

---

## I. Introduction

1. This arbitration arises out of a 30-year build-operate-transfer concession (the "Concession") granted to Claimant's local project company in 2014 for the design, financing, construction, and operation of a deepwater container terminal on the Atlantic coast of the Respondent State. The dispute concerns the lawfulness of two ministerial orders issued in March and July 2021 (the "Disputed Orders"), which together suspended Claimant's exclusive berthing rights and re-allocated approximately 38 per cent of projected throughput to a competing state-controlled operator.

2. Claimant commenced these proceedings under Article 41 of the Concession on 9 February 2022, claiming damages in the principal amount of EUR 612,400,000, together with pre- and post-award interest, costs, and a declaration that the Disputed Orders constituted a breach of the Concession's stabilisation clause (Article 27).

## II. Procedural Posture

3. The Tribunal was constituted on 22 June 2022. Procedural Order No. 1 was issued on 19 July 2022 following a case management conference held by videoconference. A hearing on jurisdiction and merits took place in Paris from 6–15 May 2023. Post-hearing briefs were filed on 4 August 2023. The Tribunal declared the proceedings closed under Article 27 of the 2021 ICC Rules on 22 September 2023.

## III. Jurisdiction

## III. Jurisdiction

4. Respondent's jurisdictional objections — principally that the Disputed Orders are acts *jure imperii* outside the contractual arbitration clause — were addressed and rejected in the Tribunal's Decision on Jurisdiction of 12 December 2022 and are not reopened here. The Tribunal reaffirms that the Concession's broad arbitration clause ("any dispute *arising out of or in connection with* this Agreement") captures regulatory acts that directly impair the Claimant's contractual entitlements, irrespective of their formal characterisation under domestic law.

## IV. Applicable Law on the Merits

## IV. Applicable Law on the Merits

5. The Concession is governed by its own terms and, on a subsidiary basis, by the laws of the Respondent State. The Tribunal has further had regard to general principles of public international law to the extent that Article 27 of the Concession expressly incorporates such principles in respect of the stabilisation undertaking.

## V. The Tribunal's Findings on Breach

## V. The Tribunal's Findings on Breach

6. The Tribunal finds, by majority (Mr. Dlamini SC dissenting in part), that the Disputed Orders, taken together, constitute a breach of Articles 14 (exclusivity), 19 (regulatory due process), and 27 (stabilisation) of the Concession. The Tribunal does not accept Respondent's contention that the Orders were a *bona fide* exercise of police power necessary to address congestion at the port; the contemporaneous record, and in particular the inter-ministerial memorandum of 11 February 2021 (Exhibit R-117), indicates that the decisive motivation was the protection of a competing state-owned operator.

7. *[Paragraphs 142–311 omitted.]*

7. *[Paragraphs 142–311 omitted.]*

## VI. Quantum

8. For the reasons set out above, the Tribunal awards Claimant the sum of **EUR 418,900,000** in damages, together with compound interest at six-month EURIBOR plus 2 per cent from 1 August 2021 until payment.

# Africa's Arbitration Renaissance: Diversity, Capacity, and the Tribunals of the Next Decade

*Global Arbitration Review — Long Feature*
*By Helena Marston, Senior Correspondent, GAR Africa Desk*
*Published 4 March 2026*

---

When the African Union's High Level Panel on Illicit Financial Flows out of Africa published its now-famous 2015 report, it estimated that the continent was losing approximately $50 billion a year to illicit outflows — a figure widely cited, occasionally contested, and broadly accepted within the African development community.

What the report did not say, but what has become increasingly clear in the decade since, is that an analogous, less publicly visible, and perhaps even larger leakage was occurring through the African investment-treaty arbitration system. Not in the form of corrupt outflows, but in the form of expertise — African disputes, often involving African parties, frequently arising out of investments in African economies, being adjudicated by tribunals containing almost no Africans.

That, finally, is changing. The change has been slow, uneven, and contested. But it is happening, and the GAR Africa Desk's twelve-month investigation into the composition of investment-treaty tribunals in Africa-related matters between 2014 and 2024 confirms it.

## A Decade of Numbers

## A Decade of Numbers

Of the 247 publicly known investment-treaty arbitrations registered between 1 January 2014 and 31 December 2023 in which the respondent State was an African State or in which the substantive dispute concerned an investment located in an African State, GAR has identified the composition of 218 tribunals.

The summary numbers tell a story.

In the five-year period 2014–2018, of the 318 tribunal seats filled in Africa-related matters for which composition data is available, 27 — or 8.5 per cent — were occupied by arbitrators who hold African nationality. Two of those 27 were women.

In the five-year period 2019–2023, the comparable figure was 71 of 339, or 20.9 per cent. Fourteen of the 71 were women.

In the five-year period 2019–2023, the comparable figure was 71 of 339, or 20.9 per cent. Fourteen of the 71 were women.

The shift is real, but it is incomplete. Sub-Saharan African nationals remain underrepresented as a proportion of arbitrator appointments relative to the proportion of Africa-related cases on the docket. African women remain particularly underrepresented. And while the trend line is encouraging, the absolute numbers — fourteen seats over five years, across an entire region of fifty-four countries — remain humblingly modest.

## The Generational Shift

Conversations with practitioners, institutional officials, and arbitrators conducted for this feature point to several drivers of the shift.

Conversations with practitioners, institutional officials, and arbitrators conducted for this feature point to several drivers of the shift.

The most frequently cited is generational. The first generation of African international arbitrators — figures such as the late Judge Kéba Mbaye of Senegal, Professor Doudou Thiam of Senegal, and Professor Mohammed Bedjaoui of Algeria — built their reputations primarily through international public-law practice, often through the International Court of Justice, the International Law Commission, or senior diplomatic appointments. Their successors operated within a smaller and more institutionally constrained pipeline.

The current generation has grown up within a more institutionalised African investment-treaty practice. Many have come through major international law firms, through the legal departments of African ministries of justice and trade, through the African Development Bank's legal team, or through the secretariats of regional arbitration institutions in Cairo, Lagos, Mauritius, and Kigali. They have, in many cases, both common-law and civil-law training. They have, increasingly, post-graduate degrees from European, North American, and African institutions.

"There used to be a feeling that to be appointed as an arbitrator in a treaty case, you had to first be appointed to a UN body," said Salim Moollan KC, who has acted as both counsel and arbitrator in numerous Africa-related matters. "That has changed. The credibility now flows the other way. People are being appointed to UN bodies because they have proven themselves as arbitrators."

## The Counsel Pipeline

## The Counsel Pipeline

A second driver is the maturation of the African counsel bar. Major international firms — Allen & Overy (now A&O Shearman), Clifford Chance, Norton Rose Fulbright, White & Case, Herbert Smith Freehills, Three Crowns, and Volterra Fietta among others — have expanded their Africa-related practices substantially over the past decade, both through London-based teams and through formal or de facto presences in African legal markets.

This has had a virtuous effect on arbitrator appointments. Counsel familiar with African parties, with African legal systems, and with African contract drafting are better placed to advocate for the appointment of African arbitrators. And African arbitrators, in turn, have come to know counsel — and to be visible to them in a way that the previous generation often was not.

"We used to be invisible," said one prominent African practitioner who asked not to be named. "Not deliberately invisible — just invisible. The pipeline did not exist. The pipeline exists now."

## The Pan-African Institutions

## The Pan-African Institutions

A third driver is the growth and increasing credibility of regional African arbitration institutions. The Lagos Court of Arbitration, the Cairo Regional Centre for International Commercial Arbitration, the Kigali International Arbitration Centre, and the Mauritius International Arbitration Centre have, over the past decade, built up serious caseloads, panels of arbitrators, and procedural infrastructure.

Of particular interest is the growth in commercial caseload at the Lagos Court of Arbitration, which registered 312 cases in 2023, up from 78 in 2018. While the LCA is principally a commercial rather than investment-treaty institution, its growth has produced a deeper pool of experienced African arbitrators with the procedural sophistication required for institutional treaty work.

The Kigali International Arbitration Centre, which is younger, has positioned itself as a hub for East African commercial work and increasingly for investment-related disputes connected to the African Continental Free Trade Area's evolving dispute-settlement architecture. The Centre's panel of arbitrators currently includes 47 practitioners, of whom 19 are women — among the strongest gender-balance ratios of any African arbitral institution.

## The Rising Practitioners

## The Rising Practitioners

A consistent theme of the practitioners interviewed for this feature was the emergence of a cohort of African investment-treaty practitioners in the mid-career bracket — broadly, those aged between 40 and 55 — who are increasingly being appointed to investment-treaty tribunals and who are likely to become the dominant African voices in the field over the coming decade.

Asked to name names, our interviewees were generous and varied. Among those most consistently mentioned — by counsel, by institutional officials, and by other arbitrators — as "rising practitioners worth watching" were:

- **Dr. Mouhamadou Ndour** (Senegal), partner at Cabinet Ndour & Associés (Dakar) and visiting professor at the Université Cheikh Anta Diop. Recently appointed as co-arbitrator in two ICSID matters involving Francophone African respondent States.
- **Ms. Funke Adekoya SAN** (Nigeria), founding partner of Æ-LEX (Lagos) and a member of the LCIA Court. Has acted as both counsel and arbitrator in numerous Africa-related matters and was the subject of GAR's *In Profile* feature in October 2024.
- **Dr. Amara N. Okonkwo** (Nigeria), London- and Lagos-based independent arbitrator with door tenancy at Gray's Inn Square Chambers. Investment-treaty and energy/mining specialist.
- **Mr. Henri Lagarde** (Côte d'Ivoire), independent arbitrator based in Paris and Abidjan, formerly of an international firm's Paris arbitration practice.
- **Dr. Tendai Biti** (Zimbabwe), formerly the Republic of Zimbabwe's Minister of Finance and now in private practice as both counsel and arbitrator in commercial and investment-treaty matters.
- **Ms. Wanjiru Kamau-Rutenberg** (Kenya), formerly director of African Women in Agricultural Research and Development and now active in arbitration and ADR practice.
- **Mr. Sipho Dlamini SC** (South Africa), senior counsel at the Johannesburg Bar with an international investment-treaty practice.
- **Dr. Sonia Nour** (Egypt), of counsel to a regional Cairo firm, with experience in both investment-treaty arbitration and ICC commercial arbitration.
- **Ms. Aïssatou Diallo** (Mali), independent arbitrator and former senior legal adviser to the African Development Bank.
- **Mr. Kwame Asare-Boateng** (Ghana), partner at a leading Accra firm and a member of the ICSID Panel of Arbitrators.
- **Dr. Lillian Mukiibi** (Uganda), formerly with the OECD Investment Division, now in private practice in Kampala and London.
- **Mr. Carlos da Silva** (Mozambique), partner at a leading Maputo firm specialising in mining and infrastructure disputes.

The list is, of course, not exhaustive. It also reflects the geographic and linguistic biases of the GAR Africa Desk's network of interlocutors. African civil-law practitioners and Lusophone practitioners are, we acknowledge, likely under-represented relative to their actual presence and capacity.

## The Counterargument

Not all of the practitioners we spoke to were uniformly optimistic. Several pointed to a continuing tendency for African appointments to be "consolation-prize appointments" — co-arbitrator appointments rather than presiding-arbitrator appointments, particularly in the highest-value matters.

A 2024 analysis published in the *Journal of International Arbitration* by Professor Olufunmi Adekoya of the University of Lagos Faculty of Law found that, of the African nationals appointed to investment-treaty tribunals in 2019–2023, only 17 per cent were appointed as presiding arbitrator. The equivalent figure for European and North American nationals was 41 per cent.

"The numbers are getting better," Professor Adekoya told us. "But they are getting better at the bottom. The chair is still mostly elsewhere."

She added: "I don't want to be a sceptic. I want to be a realist. The system is moving. It is not moving fast enough."

## The Institutional Response

She added: "I don't want to be a sceptic. I want to be a realist. The system is moving. It is not moving fast enough."

## The Institutional Response

The two principal institutional bodies — ICSID and the PCA — have, over the past several years, made deliberate efforts to address the representativeness question.

ICSID's Diversity Roster Initiative, launched in 2022, makes available to parties a curated list of qualified arbitrators from underrepresented regions and demographics. As of December 2025, the roster contained 218 names, of whom 67 were African nationals. The Centre has reported a "measurable but moderate" uptake of the roster in subsequent appointments.

The PCA has taken a less formal but, by some accounts, equally effective approach, through its Junior Lawyers programme and its quiet matchmaking work between African and other parties seeking presiding arbitrators and qualified candidates.

The major arbitration institutions outside the investment-treaty space — the ICC, the LCIA, the SCC, and the HKIAC — have also taken steps. The ICC reports that 23 per cent of its African appointments in 2023 went to African nationals, up from 8 per cent in 2018. The LCIA reports a smaller but parallel shift, from 6 per cent in 2018 to 17 per cent in 2023.

## The Capacity Question

Perhaps the deepest question — and the one to which we received the most divergent answers — concerns capacity-building.

## The Capacity Question

Perhaps the deepest question — and the one to which we received the most divergent answers — concerns capacity-building.

The optimists believe that the African counsel and arbitrator pipeline is, finally, of sufficient depth and breadth to sustain itself without further institutional intervention. They point to the steady stream of African graduates of the LLM programmes at NYU, Columbia, Harvard, Cambridge, Oxford, Geneva, Leiden, and Sciences Po, many of whom have returned to African practice. They point to the success of African Bar conferences, of African Society of International Law, and of regional moots such as the Africa Round of the Vis Moot and the Foreign Direct Investment Moot.

The pessimists believe that the pipeline is still dangerously narrow at the senior end, that too few African arbitrators have the credibility and the case experience to be appointed as president of a tribunal in a high-value treaty case, and that the gains of the past decade could be reversed if the institutional support and the deliberate counsel-side advocacy that has produced them is withdrawn.

Both perspectives have merit. The truth, as is so often the case, is probably somewhere in between.

## Looking Forward

Both perspectives have merit. The truth, as is so often the case, is probably somewhere in between.

## Looking Forward

The next decade will be decisive. The African Continental Free Trade Area's evolving investment protocol is likely to generate new institutional architecture for investor-State dispute settlement on the continent. The Pan-African Investment Code, if it is ratified by enough States to become operative, will generate a new caseload that, for reasons both substantive and political, will demand more African arbitrators.

And the underlying economics are changing. Africa-related disputes are no longer a niche corner of the investment-treaty system. They are a growing share of the docket — and they are increasingly involving African parties on both sides, not only African respondent States but also African claimants suing other African States, and African claimants suing non-African States in respect of African investments.

The system, in short, will need African arbitrators. The question is whether the system will appoint them.

The evidence of the past decade gives some reason for cautious optimism. The evidence of the present is that the work is far from done.

## A Note on Methodology

## A Note on Methodology

The GAR Africa Desk's database of Africa-related investment-treaty tribunals is compiled from publicly available information, including ICSID public case files, PCA case databases, the *Investment Treaty Arbitration Reporter*, the *Investment Treaty News*, and supplementary information provided on a confidential basis by counsel and institutional officials.

We acknowledge that our coverage is necessarily incomplete: a substantial share of investment-treaty arbitrations remain confidential, and tribunal compositions in such matters are not publicly disclosed. We estimate that we have captured approximately 88 per cent of the relevant universe for the period covered.

We are grateful to the practitioners, institutional officials, and academic commentators who shared their time and expertise with us in the preparation of this feature. Errors and omissions remain, of course, our own.

## Sidebar: Voices From the Bar

In the course of preparing this feature, the GAR Africa Desk spoke with more than 60 practitioners across 18 African and four non-African jurisdictions. The following short excerpts, all on the record except where otherwise noted, give a flavour of the range of perspectives encountered.

"What I want to see is more African presidents of tribunals. Not co-arbitrators. Presidents. The chair is the chair. The chair runs the case."
— *Adaeze Okeke, partner, Lagos*

"The progress is real and it is meaningful and we should celebrate it. But we should also be honest that the progress is uneven across regions. Francophone Africa, in particular, is still under-served."
— *Senior practitioner, Paris (background)*

"The growth of African arbitration institutions is the single most important development of the past decade. Without it, we would not have the case experience that produces credible arbitrator candidates. The institutions matter."
— *Funke Adekoya SAN, founding partner, Æ-LEX, Lagos*

"I think we still under-appreciate how much of this has been driven by client demand. African parties, particularly African sovereigns, have been more willing to ask for African arbitrators. Counsel have responded. Institutions have responded. But the demand had to come first."
— *Henri Lagarde, independent arbitrator, Abidjan and Paris*

"What I worry about is the next generation. The current generation has made the breakthrough. The next generation needs to consolidate it. And the pipeline of practitioners in their early thirties who are positioned to become serious treaty arbitrators in fifteen years is, frankly, thinner than it should be."
— *Mouhamadou Ndour, partner, Dakar*

# Goldman's Lagos Bet: Inside Amara Okonkwo's Push Into West African Infrastructure Finance

*BusinessDay (Lagos) — Weekend Cover Feature, 14 February 2026*

---

## A Long Way From Victoria Island

The view from the 17th floor of the Civic Towers building on Ozumba Mbadiwe Avenue is, if you catch it in the right light at the right time of evening, almost spectacular. The Lagos lagoon glints copper in the late sun, the snarl of traffic on the Lekki-Epe Expressway softens to a distant rumble, and the new mass-transit pylons running south toward Ajah are barely visible against the haze.

It is from this office — a corner suite that Goldman Sachs took on a ten-year lease in 2022, when the firm announced its expanded West African presence — that Amara N. Okonkwo, the bank's recently promoted Managing Director and Head of Sub-Saharan African Infrastructure Finance, has spent the past eighteen months trying to build a business that did not, until very recently, really exist.

"There has always been infrastructure money in Africa," she says, when asked how the desk has changed since she took it over in November 2024. "There has not always been infrastructure capital. Those are different problems. We are trying to solve the second one."

The distinction is one she draws often. Money, in her vocabulary, is what banks lend. Capital is what builds — patient, structured, blended, often with insurance wrapped around it and a public-sector anchor underneath. Her desk's mandate, as she explained to BusinessDay in an interview at the Civic Towers offices last week, is the latter.

## Background

Ms. Okonkwo, 41, joined Goldman Sachs in 2010 as a first-year analyst in the firm's London office, on the Emerging Markets Financing team within the Investment Banking Division. She holds a BSc in Economics from the London School of Economics (first class, 2007), an MSc in Finance from Imperial College London (2008), and an MBA from Wharton (2014, on a leave of absence from the firm).

She made vice president in 2015, executive director in 2018, and managing director in November 2024, when the firm reorganised its sub-Saharan Africa coverage into a single product-and-coverage line headquartered in Lagos. She is one of three MDs in Goldman's Lagos office and the most senior woman in the firm's sub-Saharan Africa business.

Her appointment was noted, somewhat unusually, in a brief item in the November 2024 issue of *Euromoney*, which described it as "evidence, finally, that the bulge-bracket firms are taking Lagos seriously as a regional booking centre rather than as a satellite of Johannesburg."

## The Portfolio

## The Portfolio

The desk's most visible recent transaction was the financing, announced in September 2025, of a 1,800-megawatt combined-cycle gas plant in the Ogun State free trade zone — a deal that involved Goldman as lead structuring agent for a $2.1 billion debt package syndicated across the International Finance Corporation, Standard Bank, MUFG, and the African Export-Import Bank. The transaction, which closed in early December 2025, was named "African Infrastructure Deal of the Year" by Project Finance International in its January 2026 awards.

A second transaction — the financing of an upgrade to the rail link between Apapa Port and the Tin Can Island container terminal — closed in March 2025 with Goldman as joint book-running manager on a $480 million infrastructure-bond placement underwritten under the African Trade Insurance Agency's political-risk cover.

A third, smaller but in Ms. Okonkwo's view more strategically important, was the structuring of a $145 million blended-finance facility for a fibre-optic backbone project in the Sahel — a transaction that brought together the European Investment Bank, the U.S. Development Finance Corporation, and a syndicate of three African commercial banks, with Goldman acting as financial adviser to the sponsor.

"The fibre deal will not show up in any league table," she said. "It is too small. But it is the kind of deal we have to learn to do well, because the next decade of African infrastructure finance is going to be many small deals stitched together, not one big deal that solves everything."

## On Capital Structure

Asked what surprises her most about the West African infrastructure-finance market, she paused for nearly a full minute before answering.

Asked what surprises her most about the West African infrastructure-finance market, she paused for nearly a full minute before answering.

"The mismatch," she said eventually. "Most of the available local-currency capital is short-dated. Most of the available long-dated capital is in hard currency. Almost no one wants to take the FX risk for thirty years. So we structure around it — currency hedges, partial credit guarantees, MIGA insurance, escrow accounts at offshore banks. And every time we structure around it, the deal gets more expensive, and we wonder why African infrastructure is more expensive than it should be."

Her view, expressed at a panel at the African Finance Corporation's annual conference in Kigali in October 2025, is that the development-finance community has been too slow to deploy local-currency-denominated long-dated capital at scale. She has been a vocal advocate for the African Development Bank's local-currency lending pilot and has, behind the scenes, argued that Goldman should make a more aggressive market in naira-denominated infrastructure bonds.

The firm's appetite for such an expansion remains, as one senior Goldman executive in New York put it in a recent internal call, "constructive but not enthusiastic."

## Culture and Hiring

## Culture and Hiring

The Lagos office now employs 47 people across investment banking, global markets, and operations. Ms. Okonkwo has overseen the infrastructure finance team's expansion from three professionals in late 2024 to twelve as of February 2026, with hires from Standard Chartered, the African Finance Corporation, and the World Bank Group.

Her hiring philosophy, she said, has been deliberately tilted toward what she called "structuring brains, not relationship brains."

"You can teach someone to take a CFO to dinner," she said. "You cannot teach someone to read a force-majeure clause. I would rather hire the second person and send them to dinner once a week."

The Lagos office is, by most accounts, one of the more diverse Goldman offices globally. As of December 2025, women constituted 43 per cent of the office's professional staff and 50 per cent of its managing directors — figures Ms. Okonkwo describes as "fine, but not done."

## Personal

Ms. Okonkwo was born in Enugu in 1984 and educated at Queen's College Lagos before going to LSE. She is the eldest of three children. Her father, a retired federal civil servant, lives in Enugu. Her mother, a retired secondary-school principal, lives with her in Ikoyi.

She is married to Tunji Adekoya, the chief financial officer of a Lagos-based logistics holding company. They have two children, aged 8 and 5. She has spoken publicly about her difficulty in balancing the demands of her role with parenting and has, since taking the MD title, declined all weekend speaking engagements that are not held in Lagos.

She is a non-executive director of the LSE African Development Foundation, a member of the board of trustees of the African Leadership Academy in Johannesburg, and an alumna mentor for the Wharton School's African Student Association.

She does not, she says, sit on any corporate boards in Nigeria, in order to avoid any appearance of conflict with her client coverage responsibilities at Goldman.

## On Being Mistaken for Someone Else

## On Being Mistaken for Someone Else

Ms. Okonkwo has, on at least three documented occasions, been confused in international media with an internationally prominent arbitrator of the same name and middle initial — Dr. Amara N. Okonkwo, the London- and Lagos-based investment-treaty arbitrator.

Asked about the confusion, she laughed.

"I have been asked at conferences about ICSID cases I have never read," she said. "I have been seated next to ambassadors who clearly expected someone else. I have, twice, been congratulated on awards I did not write."

She added: "I have met her. She is a wonderful woman. She is not me. I am not her. We are not related. I am told this happens to many Okonkwos."

She added: "I have met her. She is a wonderful woman. She is not me. I am not her. We are not related. I am told this happens to many Okonkwos."

The confusion has not been, in her view, professionally damaging. "Bankers and arbitrators do different things on different days," she said. "Our calendars do not really clash."

## What Comes Next

Asked where she sees the desk in five years, she was characteristically reluctant to make a forecast.

## What Comes Next

Asked where she sees the desk in five years, she was characteristically reluctant to make a forecast.

"I would like us to have closed twenty billion dollars of capital that built things that worked," she said. "I would like at least half of that to be local currency. I would like at least half of the bankers on my team to be from West Africa, trained in West Africa, and committed to staying in West Africa. If I get even half of those, I will be content."

The Lagos sky over the lagoon was, by then, turning purple, and the traffic on Ozumba Mbadiwe was starting to slow. She glanced at her watch, said something to her assistant about a 7 p.m. call with Hong Kong, and stood.

The interview was over. The desk, she reminded us politely, had not finished its day.

## Sidebar: The Ogun Combined-Cycle Gas Deal — A Closer Look

The $2.1 billion senior debt package supporting the Ogun State combined-cycle gas plant, which closed in December 2025, has been described by people involved in the transaction as one of the most structurally innovative power-sector financings ever closed in West Africa.

The transaction structure, as publicly described in lender materials:

The transaction structure, as publicly described in lender materials:

- **Sponsor equity:** $620 million from a consortium led by a Nigerian energy holding company, with minority participation by a Gulf-based infrastructure fund and the African Finance Corporation.
- **Senior debt:** $2.1 billion at financial close, structured as a syndicated facility with a 17-year tenor and a five-year availability period.
- **Mezzanine debt:** $310 million provided by a club of three African development finance institutions, with subordination and equity-conversion features.
- **Currency split:** approximately 78 per cent U.S. dollar denomination, 22 per cent naira denomination — a deliberate choice driven by the project's mixed offtake structure.
- **Tenor:** Senior tenor of 17 years, with a balloon payment of approximately 8 per cent of principal at maturity.
- **Coupon:** A floating-rate structure with a spread that, the lead arrangers say, was tighter than any comparable West African power-sector financing in the past decade.

Insurance and credit-enhancement features:

- A Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency (MIGA) political-risk insurance wrap covering approximately $640 million of the senior debt.
- A partial credit guarantee from the African Development Bank covering approximately $185 million of the naira-denominated tranche.
- A long-term offtake agreement with the Nigerian Bulk Electricity Trading Company (NBET) underpinning approximately 65 per cent of the projected revenue.

The transaction is, in Ms. Okonkwo's view, a template for what she calls "the next decade of West African power."

# Professor Amara Okonkwo and the Reframing of Igbo Dialectology

*Journal of African Languages and Linguistics — Featured Scholar Profile, Vol. 46, Issue 2 (Autumn 2025)*

---

## Introduction

When Professor Amara Okonkwo joined the Department of Linguistics at the School of Oriental and African Studies (SOAS), University of London, in 2009 as a lecturer in West African languages, the formal study of Igbo dialectology in British academic linguistics had, by general consensus, been in a slow and embarrassed retreat for nearly two decades.

The reasons were familiar enough. The canonical surveys of the language — those of Williamson (1972), Manfredi (1991), and Emenanjo (1978/2015 revised) — had partitioned the Igbo dialect continuum in ways that were demonstrably contested by speakers, often invisible in field recordings, and increasingly difficult to defend on either phonological or sociolinguistic grounds. The reference grammar tradition, meanwhile, had grown so dependent on the so-called Central Igbo standard (loosely, the variety codified by the Society for Promoting Igbo Language and Culture in the 1970s) that any departure from it was treated as deviation rather than data.

It was into this fatigued field that Professor Okonkwo arrived — a young scholar, then 32, with a PhD from the University of Edinburgh on the tonal phonology of Anambra Igbo (supervised by Peter Ladefoged's former student James Kirby), a fluency in five Igbo varieties (Owerri, Onitsha, Nsukka, Mbaise, and Ohuhu), and what one colleague at SOAS would later describe as "a positively allergic intolerance for received dialectological wisdom."

In the sixteen years since, she has produced what is now widely regarded as the most consequential rethinking of Igbo dialect classification in three generations.

## A Monograph That Has Already Acquired Its Own Bibliography

## A Monograph That Has Already Acquired Its Own Bibliography

Professor Okonkwo's *Tone, Register, and the Boundaries of Igbo: A Dialectological Reframing* (Oxford University Press, 2023; 547 pages, with three accompanying audio archives) is, by any reasonable measure, the academic event of the past three years in West African linguistics.

The monograph's central argument — that the traditional five- or six-cluster taxonomy of Igbo dialects has misread tonal microvariation as evidence of major-cluster boundaries, when in fact those boundaries are sociolinguistic artefacts of post-1970 standardisation pressures — has provoked sustained debate at every major Africanist linguistics conference since its publication.

A symposium devoted to the book at the 56th Annual Conference on African Linguistics (Yale, March 2025) ran for two full days, with over 30 invited papers. A book panel at the West African Linguistic Society meeting in Accra in August 2024 was, by all accounts, both rigorous and intermittently combative. *Language*, the flagship journal of the Linguistic Society of America, ran a 40-page review symposium in its September 2024 issue, with contributions from six leading scholars including Professor Akinbiyi Akinlabi (Rutgers), Professor Carlos Gussenhoven (Radboud), and Professor M. Eunice Adaku (University of Nigeria, Nsukka).

The reviews have not been uniformly admiring. Professor Adaku, in particular, has argued that Okonkwo's reliance on a relatively narrow set of urban consultants in Onitsha and Owerri risks reproducing precisely the kind of standardisation bias the monograph claims to dismantle. Okonkwo's reply, published in *Language* in March 2025, was characteristically pointed: "Professor Adaku is correct that our consultant base could and should be broader. She is incorrect that the conclusion changes."

## The Tonal Phonology

## The Tonal Phonology

The technical core of Okonkwo's work is her treatment of what she terms "register-mediated tonal alignment" — the observation that what previous dialectologists had transcribed as discrete high-, low-, and downstep-tone distinctions in fact patterned, in spontaneous speech across all five major Igbo varieties, as gradient register shifts mediated by phrase-level prosodic boundaries.

The argument rests on a corpus of 1,247 hours of recorded conversational Igbo collected between 2011 and 2021 in eleven Igbo-speaking communities in Nigeria. The corpus, the largest of its kind, has been deposited (with appropriate access controls) in the SOAS World Languages Archive and is available to qualified researchers under a tiered access agreement.

The acoustic analysis, conducted in collaboration with the Phonetics Laboratory at University College London, involved automated F0 extraction, manual annotation by a team of seven trained phoneticians (four of them L1 Igbo speakers), and a series of mixed-effects regression models testing for the predicted effects of phrasal position, focal stress, and discourse register on tonal realisation.

The results, as Okonkwo argues in Chapters 5–7 of the monograph, are difficult to reconcile with the traditional cluster-based account.

## A Note on Methodology

## A Note on Methodology

What has perhaps drawn the most sustained methodological commentary is Chapter 3 of the monograph, in which Okonkwo lays out what she calls a "consultant-centred dialectology" — an approach that explicitly privileges the language-internal intuitions of L1 speakers over the comparative-historical categories imposed by external description.

The chapter draws heavily on Okonkwo's own field experience but also on the philosophy of language work of Margaret Speas, Ken Hale, and (more controversially) on the indigenous-language reclamation literature of the past two decades.

Critics have argued that this commitment, while admirable, sits in tension with the highly quantitative acoustic analysis of the later chapters. Okonkwo's response, in a public lecture at SOAS in May 2025, was that the tension is not a flaw but a feature: "Linguistics that takes its speakers seriously will also take its measurements seriously. The two are not in opposition. They are in conversation."

## Teaching and Doctoral Supervision

## Teaching and Doctoral Supervision

At SOAS, Professor Okonkwo currently convenes the MA-level module "Tone and Prosody in West African Languages" (SOAS course code 156905017) and co-teaches, with Professor Lutz Marten, the long-running "Comparative Bantu Phonology" module. Her undergraduate teaching includes the second-year "Introduction to African Linguistics" course, which she took over from Professor Friederike Lüpke in 2019.

Her doctoral supervision record is, in the assessment of the SOAS doctoral school, exceptional. Of the eleven PhD candidates she has supervised to completion since 2014, seven now hold permanent positions in linguistics departments (in Nigeria, Ghana, the United Kingdom, the United States, and Germany), three have moved into senior policy or curriculum-development roles in language education in Nigeria, and one is now a junior fellow at the Max Planck Institute for Psycholinguistics in Nijmegen.

Her former student Dr. Chiamaka Nwosu, now a Reader in African Linguistics at the University of Hamburg, described Okonkwo's supervisory style in a 2023 *SOAS Linguistics Newsletter* profile as "the most generous and the most exacting I have ever encountered. She would read every footnote. She would also tell you, in writing, exactly what she thought was wrong with the footnote."

## A Brief Biographical Note

## A Brief Biographical Note

Professor Okonkwo was born in Awka, Anambra State, in 1977. She attended Federal Government Girls' College Onitsha, took her first degree in English Language and Literature at the University of Nigeria, Nsukka (1998), and her MA in African Linguistics at the University of Leiden (2002). Her PhD, awarded by the University of Edinburgh in 2008, was titled "The Phonology of Tone Spreading in Anambra Igbo: A Laboratory Study."

She joined SOAS in 2009, was promoted to senior lecturer in 2015, reader in 2019, and to a personal chair in West African Linguistics in 2022. She holds a visiting professorship at the University of Nigeria, Nsukka, and has been a visiting researcher at the Max Planck Institute for Psycholinguistics (2017), the University of California at Berkeley's Linguistics Department (2019), and the Centre for Endangered Languages Documentation at the University of Hawaii at Manoa (2023).

She is a fellow of the Linguistic Society of America (elected 2024) and a corresponding member of the West African Linguistic Society. She has served on the editorial boards of the *Journal of African Languages and Linguistics*, *Studies in African Linguistics*, and *Phonology* (the latter since 2022).

## On the Politics of Standardisation

It would be a mistake to read Professor Okonkwo's work as merely technical. Her writing on the political economy of Igbo standardisation — particularly her 2020 article in the *Journal of West African Languages*, "Whose Igbo? The standardisation question revisited" — has been read with as much attention by language activists, curriculum developers, and the Federal Ministry of Education as by academic linguists.

She has been a consistent critic of what she calls "the textbook fallacy" — the assumption that the existence of an Igbo language textbook in a particular form implies that the form represents anything more than an editorial decision made by a small committee in the 1970s.

She has also been openly sceptical of UNESCO's classification of Igbo as a language at "high risk" of disappearance — not because she disputes the language-shift evidence in urban centres, but because she believes the language-at-risk discourse has had perverse effects on Nigerian government policy, displacing investment in mother-tongue education in favour of what she calls "language-preservation theatre."

"You do not save a language by mourning it," she said in a 2024 keynote at the African Language Atlas conference in Yaoundé. "You save it by speaking it, teaching it, and arguing about it."

## A Common Confusion

## A Common Confusion

Professor Okonkwo's publication record on Google Scholar and several other indexing services has, on occasion, been confused with that of a separately distinguished Amara N. Okonkwo who works as an international arbitrator based between London and Lagos.

The two have, to the best of our knowledge, never met. They share a first name, a surname, and (in one case) a middle initial. They do not share a profession, a research agenda, or a publication list. Confusion of the two has led to at least one citation indexing error in *African Studies Review* (since corrected) and a memorable incident at the British Library in 2022 in which a reference librarian, having been asked to retrieve "the Okonkwo monograph on Igbo tone," produced a bound volume of selected investment-treaty awards.

Professor Okonkwo is reported to have laughed.

## Current Projects

Her current research, supported by a five-year European Research Council Advanced Grant (€2.45 million, 2024–2029), titled "Reanalysing the West Benue-Congo dialect continuum: a comparative laboratory phonology approach," extends the methodological framework of her Igbo work to a broader comparative project covering Igbo, Edo, Yoruba, and Igala.

The project will produce, by 2029, what she has described as "the first phonological atlas of West Benue-Congo that is built on speakers rather than on stereotypes."

The first project workshop was held at SOAS in November 2024. The second is scheduled for the University of Ibadan in July 2026.
